questions.question: Order the historical anchors of PFC research chronologically:
items: Brodmann (1912) estimates PFC ~30% of human cortex
Franz Gall proposes phrenology (early 1800s)
Hitzig & Fritsch (late 1860s) stimulate posterior frontal cortex
Semendeferi (1997, 2002) revises PFC scaling to ~35% in humans and great apes
correct_order: Franz Gall proposes phrenology (early 1800s)
Hitzig & Fritsch (late 1860s) stimulate posterior frontal cortex
Brodmann (1912) estimates PFC ~30% of human cortex
Semendeferi (1997, 2002) revises PFC scaling to ~35% in humans and great apes
questions.explanation: The historical lineage runs Gall (phrenology, early 1800s) → Hitzig & Fritsch (1860s motor stimulation) → Brodmann (1912 PFC scaling estimates) → Semendeferi (1997/2002 MRI revision showing humans and great apes have similar proportional PFC).

questions.question: Order the steps of Miller & Cohen's (2001) switch-operator metaphor in action when a phone rings:
items: Behaviour: ignore the phone (or answer it)
PFC sets up the appropriate pattern of information flow for the context
Phone rings — sensory input enters the system
Context evaluated: 'I am at home' vs 'I am at the neighbour's'
correct_order: Phone rings — sensory input enters the system
Context evaluated: 'I am at home' vs 'I am at the neighbour's'
PFC sets up the appropriate pattern of information flow for the context
Behaviour: ignore the phone (or answer it)
questions.explanation: Miller & Cohen's switch-operator: input arrives → context is identified → PFC strengthens the appropriate pathways and inhibits others → context-appropriate behaviour emerges (answer at home, ignore at the neighbour's).

questions.question: Order the trial structure of the Wallis, Anderson & Miller (2001) rule-coding experiment:
items: Test 1 — respond if cued rule says 'match'
Sample image presented
Cue indicates rule (juice → matching, tone → non-matching)
Delay
Test 2
correct_order: Sample image presented
Cue indicates rule (juice → matching, tone → non-matching)
Delay
Test 1 — respond if cued rule says 'match'
Test 2
questions.explanation: Wallis et al.'s trial structure: sample → rule cue (juice or tone) → delay → test 1 → test 2. Single-unit recordings during this sequence showed rule-selective firing in lateral PFC neurons, independent of cue and stimulus.

questions.question: Order the lateral PFC topography from posterior to anterior (Badre & D'Esposito):
items: Mid-dlPFC: task switching, complex S-R contingencies
Premotor: response selection, rule matching
Frontopolar cortex: tracking goals/sub-goals, relational integration
Posterior lateral PFC: response sequences, rule learning
correct_order: Premotor: response selection, rule matching
Posterior lateral PFC: response sequences, rule learning
Mid-dlPFC: task switching, complex S-R contingencies
Frontopolar cortex: tracking goals/sub-goals, relational integration
questions.explanation: Badre & D'Esposito's policy abstraction hierarchy maps a rostral-caudal gradient: premotor (concrete S-R) → posterior lateral PFC (sequences) → mid-dlPFC (task switching) → frontopolar (goals, branching, relational integration) — least to most abstract.

questions.question: Order the Funahashi, Bruce & Goldman-Rakic (1989) oculomotor delayed response trial:
items: Saccade to remembered location
Target flashes at one of 8 spatial locations
3-second delay (no stimulus, no movement)
dlPFC neurons fire continuously through delay (delay-period activity)
correct_order: Target flashes at one of 8 spatial locations
3-second delay (no stimulus, no movement)
dlPFC neurons fire continuously through delay (delay-period activity)
Saccade to remembered location
questions.explanation: The oculomotor delayed response task: target appears → 3-s delay with no stimulus → during the delay, dlPFC neurons sustain content-specific firing (memory fields) → monkey saccades to the remembered location. Errors followed breakdowns of delay-period firing.

questions.question: Order the conflict-monitoring sequence in the original Kerns et al. (2004) account:
items: Behavioural adjustment on trial N+1
Conflict on trial N (e.g., incongruent Stroop)
dlPFC adjusts attention/control on trial N+1
ACC activation signals conflict on trial N
correct_order: Conflict on trial N (e.g., incongruent Stroop)
ACC activation signals conflict on trial N
dlPFC adjusts attention/control on trial N+1
Behavioural adjustment on trial N+1
questions.explanation: Kerns et al. (2004) framed ACC as a conflict detector that hands off to lateral PFC: incongruent trial → ACC signals → dlPFC implements control on the next trial → behaviour adjusts. Fellows & Farah (2005) later challenged this with lesion data.

questions.question: Order the dmPFC posterior-to-anterior gradient by abstraction (Venkatraman et al. 2009):
items: Anterior dmPFC: strategy-related control (deviate from baseline)
Middle dmPFC: decision-related control (face attractiveness)
Posterior dmPFC: response-related control (counting Stroop)
correct_order: Posterior dmPFC: response-related control (counting Stroop)
Middle dmPFC: decision-related control (face attractiveness)
Anterior dmPFC: strategy-related control (deviate from baseline)
questions.explanation: Venkatraman et al. (2009) ran three conflict tasks and showed control demands map onto a posterior-to-anterior dmPFC gradient (response → decision → strategy), running least-abstract to most-abstract — mirroring the dlPFC gradient.

questions.question: Order the arc of conflict-monitoring theory chronologically:
items: Behrens et al. (2007) — dmPFC tracks volatility / expectation mismatch
Fellows & Farah (2005) — dorsal-ACC lesion patients have normal Stroop
Posner & Petersen (1990) — three control systems including conflict detection
Kerns et al. (2004) — ACC N → dlPFC N+1
correct_order: Posner & Petersen (1990) — three control systems including conflict detection
Kerns et al. (2004) — ACC N → dlPFC N+1
Fellows & Farah (2005) — dorsal-ACC lesion patients have normal Stroop
Behrens et al. (2007) — dmPFC tracks volatility / expectation mismatch
questions.explanation: The theoretical arc: Posner & Petersen propose the framework (1990), Kerns et al. supply fMRI support (2004), Fellows & Farah's lesion data challenges it (2005), and Behrens et al. reframe dmPFC as a learning/volatility signal (2007).

questions.question: Order the working-memory retrieval pipeline under the distributed-network view:
items: Sensory cortex (e.g., V1-V4) sustains content-specific activity
PFC sends top-down signals to keep representations online
Stimulus disappears, but task requires holding it
Behavioural response uses the maintained representation
correct_order: Stimulus disappears, but task requires holding it
PFC sends top-down signals to keep representations online
Sensory cortex (e.g., V1-V4) sustains content-specific activity
Behavioural response uses the maintained representation
questions.explanation: The distributed network view: when the stimulus disappears, PFC sends top-down signals back to the sensory regions that processed it; those regions sustain content-specific activity (Harrison & Tong 2009 decoded orientation from V1-V4); the behavioural response then draws on the maintained representation.

questions.question: Order the EF taxonomy hierarchy from rule mechanics to coordinating systems:
items: Working memory — maintenance and manipulation keep rules online
Inhibiting inappropriate rules / shifting / relating rules
Initiating new rules
Contextual control — monitoring the environment to know when rules apply
correct_order: Initiating new rules
Inhibiting inappropriate rules / shifting / relating rules
Contextual control — monitoring the environment to know when rules apply
Working memory — maintenance and manipulation keep rules online
questions.explanation: Figure 13.1 organises EF along a logical chain: first the rule is initiated, then it can be inhibited / shifted / related to others, then contextual control monitors when it applies, and working memory holds the whole apparatus online — rules + control + WM working together.
